title: Revisiting the surface density of states of midgap Andreev edge states
authors: Gota Sato, Yasushi Nagato, Seiji Higashitani
abstract: We revisit the effect of surface roughness on midgap Andreev edge states (MAES) in p- and d- wave superconductors. For a perfectly specular surface, MAES form a flat band at the Fermi energy, which manifests as a sharp midgap peak in the surface density of states (SDOS). Previous theoretical studies have shown that MAES in p- and d-wave superconductors respond markedly differently to surface roughness. In the d-wave state, diffuse surface scattering significantly broadens the midgap peak in the SDOS, accompanied by the emergence of a V-shaped structure centered at the Fermi energy. In contrast, the midgap peak in the p-wave state remains robust against diffuse scattering. In this work, we clarify the physical origin of this contrasting behavior. A key aspect of our analysis is that the flat band in the d-wave state consists of two distinct types of MAES modes. We show that inter-mode diffuse scattering leads to substantial broadening of the midgap peak and to the formation of the V-shaped structure. By contrast, the robustness of MAES in the p-wave state arises from the presence of a single MAES mode in the flat band. These results provide new insight into the response of MAES to surface roughness.
summary: This paper explains why the zero-energy peak in p-wave superconductors remains sharp despite surface roughness, unlike in d-wave superconductors where it broadens into a V-shape. The difference is attributed to the fact that d-wave states involve scattering between two different edge modes, whereas p-wave states involve only a single mode.
main_problem: The paper investigates the physical origin of why Midgap Andreev Edge States (MAES) in p-wave superconductors are robust against surface roughness, while those in d-wave superconductors undergo significant broadening and develop a V-shaped density of states.
main_result: The authors show that d-wave fragility arises from inter-mode scattering between two distinct MAES modes, whereas p-wave robustness is due to the presence of only a single MAES mode, which prevents mode-mixing broadening.
method: The study uses the quasiclassical theory of superconductivity via the Eilenberger equation and a random S-matrix model to simulate diffuse surface scattering.
model_or_system: The system consists of 2D nonchiral p-wave and d-wave superconductors with a surface at y=0, modeled using a uniform gap approximation and a random S-matrix to represent varying degrees of surface roughness.
key_observables: Surface Density of States (SDOS) and the midgap peak at the Fermi energy.
important_parameters: Roughness parameter W (ranging from 0 for specular to 1 for diffuse), specular reflection probability R, and the gap symmetry index m.
main_assumptions: The Fermi surface is assumed to be isotropic (circular), the surface is macroscopically flat, and the analysis focuses on the nonchiral, flat-band regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 relates roughness W to reflection probability R; Figure 2 shows the angular dependence of the gap sign; Figure 3 shows numerical SDOS for d-wave; Figure 7 shows the increase of scattering parameters with W in p-wave states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MAES sensitivity to roughness, establishes a mathematical framework using Eilenberger equations and S-matrix theory, performs a comparative analysis of p-wave and d-wave modes, and concludes by identifying inter-mode scattering as the mechanism for d-wave broadening.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Roughness parameter W as a function of the specular reﬂection probability R.
FIG. 2. Angular dependence of ∆φ for (a) p-wave and (b) d-wave SC states. The |1⟩and |2⟩MAES modes exist in the regions ∆φ > 0 (sφ = +1) and ∆φ < 0 (sφ = −1), respectively.

FIG. 3. SDOS n(φ, E) in d-wave superconductors with W = 0 (R = 1), W = 0.01 (R ≃0.92), and W = 0.1 (R ≃0.5). The top panels correspond to φ = π/4, and the bottom panels correspond to φ = 3π/8. All results are calculated using the self-consistently determined ∆(y) shown in Fig. 4.

FIG. 4. Spatial dependence of the self-consistent ∆(y) in d-wave superconductors for W = 0, W = 0.01, and W = 0.1. The results are calculated at T = 0.2Tc, where Tc is the transition temperature. The distance y from the surface is scaled by the coherence length ξ = ℏvF /∆(∞).

Figure 5 shows S as a function of E/∆(∞) for W = 0.01. The left panel presents the exact result obtained within the uniform gap model, while the right panel demonstrates that the low-energy behavior is well repro- duced by the approximation in Eq. (44).
